era: 1994. Alt Metal. E=10 -- THE ABSOLUTE CEILING, FIRST OCCURRENCE IN SUMMIT. U-District, Seattle, Washington. A&M Records. Superunknown. The most complete rock album of the 1990s.
members: Ben Shepherd (bass, Superunknown era, replaced Hiro Yamamoto and Jason Everman)
key_albums: Superunknown (1994, A&M, Michael Beinhorn) -- E=10. The engine's absolute maximum. 70 minutes, 15 tracks. #1 Billboard 200. 2 Grammy wins (Black Hole Sun, Spoonman). The definitive statement of alt metal at its absolute peak.
Badmotorfinger (1991, A&M) -- predecessor, near-ceiling. Rusty Cage, Outshined, Jesus Christ Pose.
Ultramega OK (1988, SST) -- SST era, early development.
Louder Than Love (1989, A&M) -- first major label, transition record.

theory_nodes.concept: Superunknown (1994, A&M, Michael Beinhorn): released March 8 1994. Debuted #1 Billboard 200. 5M US / 9M worldwide. Two Grammy wins. 70 minutes, 15 tracks. Covers more sonic territory than any rock album of the era: 'Spoonman' (tuned-percussion world-music groove) + 'Black Hole Sun' (psychedelic slow-motion dread) + 'My Wave' (riff power) + 'Fell on Black Days' (devastating emotional directness) + 'Like Suicide' (9-minute orchestral metal). Consistent excellence across the entire 70 minutes -- exceptionally rare. The sequencing from 'Let Me Drown' to 'Like Suicide' is one of the strongest album sequences of the decade.
theory_nodes.description: Superunknown (1994) -- the record that earns E=10.

theory_nodes.concept: Chris Cornell vocal approach on Superunknown: the most complete vocal performance on any rock album of the era. Cornell had a reported range of four octaves. On Superunknown he deploys the full range with compositional intelligence: the upper register for intensity and pleading ('Black Hole Sun' chorus), the lower register for dread and inevitability ('Like Suicide'), the midrange for rhythmic riff-vocal interplay ('My Wave'). The voice is not merely powerful; it is compositionally specific -- each register serves the song's emotional architecture.
theory_nodes.description: Chris Cornell -- four-octave range deployed with compositional intelligence on Superunknown.

theory_nodes.concept: Kim Thayil guitar approach on Superunknown: dropped tunings, sitar influences (Spoonman's riff structure), dissonant harmonics, and conventional alt metal crunch -- all on the same album. Thayil is the sonic architect of the Superunknown palette; his guitar vocabulary on this record is wider than on any previous Soundgarden album. The dropped tunings give the heavy tracks their characteristic low-end density; the sitar-influenced approaches give the more experimental tracks their distinctiveness.
theory_nodes.description: Kim Thayil -- the widest guitar vocabulary on any Soundgarden record.

theory_nodes.concept: The E=10 calibration for Superunknown: cultural impact + sonic achievement simultaneously. The album was #1 commercially (cultural maximum in the grunge era), won Grammy Awards (critical recognition), and contains music of genuine artistic complexity ('Like Suicide', 'Mailman', 'Limo Wreck'). E=10 requires all dimensions simultaneously: commercial maximum, critical recognition, sonic complexity, emotional range, duration. Superunknown achieves all of them.
theory_nodes.description: Why Superunknown is E=10 -- all dimensions at maximum simultaneously.

theory_nodes.concept: The context of March 1994: Superunknown was released March 8, 1994. Kurt Cobain died April 5, 1994. Superunknown entered the public consciousness simultaneously with the end of Nirvana. The album's commercial peak coincided with the grunge generation's most significant cultural event. This temporal coincidence is not analytically decisive, but it gives the album a particular weight in the retrospective assessment of the era -- it is the last major artistic statement of the first phase of grunge, released in the final weeks before everything changed.
theory_nodes.description: March 1994 -- Superunknown and the temporal context.

theory_nodes.concept: Chris Cornell and the summit: Cornell died May 18, 2017, at age 52. The engine documents the music with factual accuracy and genuine respect. Superunknown is an artistic achievement of the first order, and Cornell's voice -- the specific instrument deployed across those 70 minutes in 1994 -- is one of the most extraordinary in rock history. The entry documents the music, the album, the achievement. Handle with respect.
theory_nodes.description: Chris Cornell -- documented with accuracy and respect.

cross_references.relationship: E=9 rises to E=10. The Melvins' sludge foundation (E=9) precedes Soundgarden's alt metal maximum (E=10). Soundgarden acknowledged the Melvins as genre predecessors in the Seattle heavy tradition.

Both E=10. Both Seattle alt metal. Contemporaneous but distinct: Superunknown is the maximalist statement (70 minutes, 15 tracks, commercial breakthrough) while Dirt is the minimalist descent (57 minutes, 13 tracks, autobiographical). Two E=10 documents from the same scene in consecutive years.

Both E=10. Summit completes at degree 348. Nirvana's Nevermind (1991) preceded Superunknown by three years and detonated the commercial breakthrough; Superunknown (1994) is the artistic culmination of the era Nevermind created.

E=10 + E=10 = E=20. The second E=20 in the summit. Soundgarden's Superunknown (the most complete rock album of the 1990s) paired with the Cascades Full Storm (the mountain range's entire acoustic spectrum from summit to sea). Both are the maximum expression of their respective categories at simultaneous E=10.

sps_pair.pairing_note: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Summit's second E=20. Superunknown's 70-minute arc from summit to depth ('Let Me Drown' to 'Like Suicide') mirrors the Cascades storm's summit-to-sea continuum (Rainier summit at 14,411 ft through alpine/subalpine/forested slopes to Puget Sound surge).

milestones: ABSOLUTE CEILING FIRST REACHED IN SUMMIT
FIRST S36 E=10 IN THE FINAL FOUR
summit_arc: 345 Melvins E=9 → 346 Soundgarden E=10 (FIRST CEILING) → 347 AIC E=10 → 348 Nirvana E=10. The summit reaches the absolute maximum at degree 346.
